data.title: ADMA Biologics drops amid short report from Culper Research
data.description:
• ADMA Biologics (ADMA) fell 15% amid a short report from Culper Research. 
  • Culper Research disclosed that it's short shares of ADMA Biologics (ADMA). 
  • ADMA (ADMA) didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's email request for comment.

data.title: TransAlta upgraded at National Bank Financial on path to double-digit EBITDA growth
data.description:
TransAlta (TAC) up 6.5% in Tuesday's trading as National Bank Financial upgraded the Canadian power generator to Outperform from Sector Perform with a C$22 price target, saying the company has a number of catalysts ahead of it that can push the stock forward.

The stock's recent 22% pullback no longer reflects the value of TransAlta's (TAC) upcoming phase 1 Keephills data center project, the US$600M Centralia gas conversion, or a recovery in Alberta power prices toward the mid-C$70s to mid-C$80s MWh range, National Bank analyst Patrick Kenny said.

At its investor day on Monday, TransAlta (TAC) outlined a path to double-digit EBITDA growth through 2029, supported by 900-1,300 MW of expected Alberta net load growth that could push pool prices to $85-$120/MWh and drive EBITDA to $1.35B-$1.8B, Kenny noted.

The anticipated increase in free cash flow and growing debt capacity should further support financial flexibility for TransAlta (TAC) to pursue a broad mix of greenfield, brownfield, and accretive M&A opportunities, the analyst said.

data.title: Relmada wins bullish view at Piper Sandler on cancer therapy
data.description:
Relmada Therapeutics (RLMD) traded higher on Tuesday after Piper Sandler launched its coverage with an Overweight recommendation, arguing that shares of the Florida-based biotech have yet to reflect the full potential of its lead cancer candidate, NDV-01.

An intravesical formulation of chemotherapy, gemcitabine, and docetaxel, NDV-01, is currently undergoing mid-stage development as a treatment for non-muscle invasive bladder cancer ((NMIBC)).

“RLMD shares seem to currently reflect minimal value for NDV-01 despite a clear value proposition in the large NMIBC market; high translatability of the existing gem/doce evidence, in our view; and cash through 2029,” analyst Kelsey Goodwin wrote.

She argued that the drug, designed to enable the gradual release of gemcitabine and docetaxel over 10 days, offers several key advantages over existing options, including a sustained-release formulation for increased exposure.

The analyst issued a $12 per share target for RLMD, assuming 20% and 15% peak market share for NDV-01 in BCG-unresponsive high-risk NMIBC and intermediate-risk NMIBC, at a probability of success of 35% and 25%, respectively.

data.title: Synopsys partners with Arm to build CPUs for data centers
data.description:
Synopsys (SNPS) partnered with Arm (ARM) to produce the UK-based company's new AGI CPU, which was designed for data centers.

Synopsys provided electronic design automation, interface IP, and hardware-assisted verification during the process. 

"Designing data center silicon for increasingly complex AI workloads requires rigorous validation across the full system," said Mohamed Awad, executive vice president of Arm's Cloud AI Business Unit. "The Arm AGI CPU reflects the strength of our SoC design and the effectiveness of our collaboration with Synopsys. Their design, IP, and verification solutions supported the development and validation of our breakthrough performance-per-watt chip for next-generation AI infrastructure."

The new CPU for data centers means Arm will be competing in the space with chipmakers such as Nvidia (NVDA), Intel (INTC), and AMD (AMD). Meta Platforms (META) will be one of the initial major customers of the new chip, which will be produced by Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM).

data.title: Private credit unlikely to cause 2008-style systemic crisis - former Bear Stearns CEO
data.description:
Alan Schwartz, who led Bear Stearns when it collapsed during the 2008 financial crisis, said Monday he does not believe the current turmoil in private credit markets will create the same systemic risks that nearly brought down the banking system 18 years ago.

“I don’t think it’s gonna be as systemic as what happened with the mortgage markets, primarily because what happened leading up to the crisis was the assets that spasmed were heavily inside the banking system,” Schwartz said in an interview on CNBC.

Schwartz, now executive chairman at Guggenheim Partners, warned that private credit still poses significant risks that require close monitoring. He noted that after the 2008 crisis, stricter capital rules pushed corporate lending outside the banking system into private credit funds and insurance companies.

“Anytime you have a very significant rise in the volume of illiquid assets without transparent transaction values, they have the potential to create spasms in the financial markets,” he said.

Schwartz pointed to growing concerns about redemption requests exceeding what funds are willing to return to investors. He also highlighted the rise of “shadow defaults,” where struggling companies opt to defer payments rather than pay cash when they lack the ability to do so.

data.title: First Majestic Silver upgraded at BMO on 'historically attractive valuation with catalysts'
data.description:
First Majestic Silver (AG) up 3.7% in Tuesday's trading as BMO Capital upgraded to Outperform from Market Perform with a C$35 price target, viewing the shares as discounted compared to historical trading multiples.

First Majestic (AG) trades at 2.2x net asset value and 10x next-12-month cash flow from operations at the bank's updated commodity price deck, compared to historical multiples often exceeding 3x NAV and 15x cash flow, BMO's Kevin O'Halloran calculated.

The analyst also pointed to "value-surfacing catalysts" in 2026 including expanding processing capacity at the Santa Elena and Gatos mines and evaluating restart potential at Jerritt Canyon and development of newly discovered deposits at Santa Elena.

data.title: Arm unveils data center CPU as it looks to take on Intel, AMD
data.description:
At its Arm Everywhere event in San Francisco, Arm Holdings (ARM) took the wraps off its long-awaited custom-designed CPU, aimed at the data center market.

Known as the Arm AGI CPU, the newly announced chip is aimed at agentic AI workloads. It can deliver more than twice the performance per rack when compared to traditional x86 platforms made by Intel (INTC) and AMD (AMD), the company said in a statement.

The Arm AGI CPU has up to 136 V3 cores per CPU and is a system-on-a-chip, offering 6 GB/s memory per core at sub-100ns latency. It's also efficient, the company said, as it uses around 300 watts of electricity, supports 1U server chassis' air-cooled deployments with up to 8,160 cores per rack, and liquid-cooled systems that can deliver more than 45,000 cores per rack.

Arm added that the CPU was developed in concert with Meta Platforms (META), which will be its first major customer for the chips. It will be produced by Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM). The company also noted that companies such as Cerebras, Cloudflare (NET), F5 (FFIV), OpenAI (OPENAI), Positron, Rebellions, SAP (SAP), and SK Telecom will deploy the Arm AGI CPU for key agentic CPU use cases.

“AI has fundamentally redefined how computing is built and deployed. Agentic computing is accelerating that change,” said Arm CEO Rene Haas in a statement. “Today marks the next phase of the Arm compute platform and a defining moment for our company. With the expansion into delivering production silicon with our Arm AGI CPU, we are giving partners more choices all built on Arm’s foundation of high-performance, power-efficient computing, to support agentic AI infrastructure at global scale.”

Arm added that it is partnering with companies such as ASRock Rack, Lenovo, Quanta Computer, and Supermicro for CPU availability. Early systems are available now, and broader availability is expected in the second half of the year, the company said.

data.title: Altria Group rebound after six-session slide
data.description:
Shares of Altria Group (MO) rose 0.84% to $64.93 in the afternoon trade on Tuesday, snapping six straight sessions of decline.

The stock has fallen around 4.7% between March 16 and March 23 compared to a 1.77% decline in the S&P 500 during the same period.

The decline comes amid weakness in U.S. consumer staples and a rotation out of defensive stocks. Investors are reassessing high valuations and softer profit outlooks across the sector. Within tobacco, declines reflect ongoing structural headwinds, including falling cigarette volumes, slower growth in smoke‑free alternatives, and market share pressures, compounding broader market losses.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system, MO is rated a Hold, with a score of 3.31 out of 5, receiving an A+ for profitability but a D- in terms of growth.

A recent Seeking Alpha analysis maintained a “hold” rating on Altria Group, citing ongoing revenue declines, market share erosion, and structural headwinds in its core business. The analyst noted that “the overall decline that the company has demonstrated is particularly discouraging because it means that, relative to other comparable offerings out there, it is struggling disproportionately,” and added that “another problem for the company is that its oral tobacco products really aren't selling well either.”

While on Wall Street, five of 14 analysts rate the stock with a strong buy, seven recommend a hold, and two of them rate it with a sell or lower.

Shares have gained around 5.5% in the past month but remain down about 12.3% year-to-date.

data.title: Freshpet shares rocked by The Farmer's Dog launch at Walmart
data.description:
Shares of Freshpet (FRPT) are on the defensive and have retreated by as much as 17% in reaction to news that a competitor is bringing their fresh dog food to Walmart (WMT).

Beginning in April, pet owners will be able to purchase The Farmer’s Dog fresh dog food at Walmart.com, expanding into a category currently dominated by Blue Buffalo (GIS) and Freshpet (FRPT).

While Freshpet (FRPT) differs from The Farmer’s Dog—selling refrigerated products versus The Farmer’s Dog’s frozen—and leads the category in total revenue and market share, The Farmer’s Dog is gaining ground and is recognized as the leading direct-to-consumer brand. 

The rivalry between the two intensified last week when Freshpet (FRPT) was accused by The Farmer’s Dog of misrepresenting its dog food as “human grade.” The BBB National Programs’ National Advertising Division ((NAD)) recommended Freshpet (FRPT) discontinue these claims and modify their advertising.

Accordingly, Freshpet (FRPT) informed NAD that it would change the claim in a commercial, “How does Freshpet make healthy food for dogs? The same way you make healthy food for people” by removing the second sentence.

The outcome weighed on shares of Freshpet (FRPT), extending a six-day losing streak for the stock.

data.title: Smithfield signals $1.325B–$1.475B adjusted operating profit target for 2026 while advancing major Sioux Falls investment
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Smithfield Foods (SFD) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Shane Smith, President and CEO, described 2025 as "an outstanding year" with "record profits, expanded margins and increased cash flow," emphasizing that "we set the foundation for multiyear growth while maintaining a very strong financial position, investing in our business and returning value to our shareholders."
  • Smith highlighted the company's return to the U.S. equity markets via IPO, ongoing transformation to a "leaner, more profitable and strategically focused company," and the streamlining of Packaged Meats, exit from non-core operations, and acceleration of automation.
  • Smith announced Donovan Owens as President of North America Pork, noting Fresh Pork segment adjusted operating profit increased to $209 million in 2025 from $30 million in 2022, and that Owens will oversee Fresh Pork, Hog Production, Commodity Risk Management, and Mexico operations.
  • Smith stated, "On a consolidated basis, adjusted operating profit increased 30% to $1.3 billion, with profit margin expanding to 8.6%, up from 7.2% in 2024." He added, "Packaged Meats delivered its fourth consecutive year of operating profit above $1 billion and its second highest profit year despite higher raw material costs."
  • Smith revealed a definitive agreement to acquire Nathan's Famous for $102 per share, expecting the deal to be "immediately accretive" and to "secure a core national brand." He also announced plans to invest up to $1.3 billion over the next 3 years in a new processing facility in Sioux Falls, South Dakota.
  • Mark Hall, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "Total company sales increased 7% for the fourth quarter and 10% for the year with growth across all segments." He added, "Record fourth quarter adjusted operating profit of $402 million fueled our record full year 2025 adjusted operating profit of $1.3 billion."

Outlook

  • Hall explained that the outlook for fiscal 2026 is for "continued margin expansion" and "total company sales to be up low single digits compared to fiscal 2025."
  • Segment adjusted operating profit guidance for 2026 is: Packaged Meats $1.1 billion to $1.2 billion, Fresh Pork $200 million to $260 million, Hog Production $150 million to $200 million, and total company adjusted operating profit in the range of $1.325 billion to $1.475 billion.
  • Hall said, "Our outlook reflects 53 weeks of operations in 2026 and does not include the impact of the proposed Nathan's Famous acquisition and investment in the new processing facilities in Sioux Falls, South Dakota."

Financial Results

  • Adjusted diluted EPS for Q4 was $0.83 per share, up from $0.52 in 2024, and full-year EPS was $2.55.
  • Full year adjusted net income from continuing operations was $1 billion.
  • Packaged Meats delivered adjusted operating profit of $1.1 billion and a margin of 12.4%, with sales of $8.8 billion (up 5.3%).
  • Fresh Pork segment reported $209 million in adjusted operating profit and $8.3 billion in sales (up 6%).
  • Hog Production posted $176 million in adjusted operating profit on sales of $3.4 billion (up 13%).
  • Net debt to adjusted EBITDA was 0.3x, and liquidity stood at $3.8 billion with $1.5 billion in cash and equivalents.
  • Dividend payments totaled $1 per share in 2025, and a quarterly dividend of $0.3125 per share was announced.

Q&A

  • Megan Christine Alexander, Morgan Stanley: Asked about Packaged Meats guidance and margin puts and takes—Mark Hall clarified the top line includes $230 million of one-time inventory sales in 2025, and Steven France explained that while raw material costs remain elevated, "we are better positioned than most companies due to the family of brands and also the extensive product portfolio that we have."
  • Alexander followed up on Hog Production guidance and supply—Smith said, "we don't see right now any material level of expansion taking place outside of productivity and improvements in health... we have seen some real structural changes in our business."
  • Benjamin Theurer, Barclays: Inquired about Fresh Pork risks and opportunities—Smith and Donovan Owens stressed focus on value-added products and connecting branding across segments, with Owens stating, "growing our Fresh Pork in that arena is going to be pivotal in 2026, as it was in 2025."
  • Leah Jordan, Goldman Sachs: Sought detail on Packaged Meats margin cadence—France explained the importance of quality merchandising and that profitability is expected to be "fairly equal from a profitability standpoint" across halves. Hall clarified the extra week is included in guidance.
  • Jordan followed up on Hog Production and feed assumptions—Smith reported "we are seeing some increases" in feed costs, but the company is "in a pretty good position" with hedging.
  • Christopher Downing, BofA Securities: Asked about Nathan's Famous accretion—Smith said, "we do believe that the transaction will be immediately accretive to our earnings."
  • Max Andrew Gumport, BNP: Probed Sioux Falls facility benefits—Smith highlighted anticipated efficiency gains and transformative impact, calling it "one of the largest single investments in American agriculture."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was constructive but focused on input cost headwinds, margin sustainability, and execution risks, pressing for detail on margin cadence, guidance assumptions, and accretion from acquisitions.
  • Management confidence remained high throughout, with Smith and Hall repeatedly describing the outlook as "comfortable" and expressing enthusiasm for strategic initiatives. Phrases like "we are confident in the outlook" and "our business model has never been stronger" reflected strong conviction.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more assertive and forward-looking, buoyed by record results and the Nathan's acquisition, while analysts maintained a measured, probing stance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was raised from the previous quarter, with the total company adjusted operating profit target increasing from a prior range of $1.225 billion–$1.325 billion to $1.325 billion–$1.475 billion.
  • Management emphasized expanded margin targets, a new major capital project (Sioux Falls facility), and the Nathan’s Famous acquisition, signaling greater strategic ambition.
  • Key financial metrics such as EPS, net income, and operating profit improved, and market share gains in core categories were highlighted.
  • Management’s confidence grew more pronounced compared to Q3, with a heightened focus on innovation, automation, and portfolio optimization. Analysts continued to focus on cost risks and the sustainability of momentum, echoing themes from the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Hall cited "continued cautious consumer spending environment and a dynamic geopolitical environment" as headwinds.
  • Geopolitical risks around Iran and their impact on fuel, corn prices, and petroleum-derived supplies were noted as areas being monitored.
  • Smith acknowledged volatility in input costs and potential feed price increases, but stated, "we think, again, that guidance that we issued encompasses that variability that we think we'll see in corn."
  • Analysts repeatedly questioned margin resilience, the impact of raw material costs, and timing of efficiency gains.

Final Takeaway

Smithfield Foods closed the year with record profitability and robust financial health, underpinned by a diversified portfolio, operational excellence, and disciplined capital allocation. The company’s strategic priorities remain focused on margin expansion, innovation, and network optimization, bolstered by the planned Nathan’s Famous acquisition and a transformative investment in Sioux Falls. Management projects another year of increased profitability in 2026, with guidance reflecting broad-based growth and continued cost discipline, while monitoring consumer trends and geopolitical developments closely.
